items.title: CMS finalizes changes to No Surprises Act dispute resolution process - Fierce Healthcare
items.reason: This item directly addresses CMS finalization of changes to the No Surprises Act's Independent Dispute Resolution process, which is a core component of the rulemaking and implementation covered by the topic.
items.summary: CMS has finalized changes to the No Surprises Act's dispute resolution process, which governs how payers and providers resolve payment disputes outside of the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) system. The finalized modifications affect the procedural and operational framework for dispute resolution under the federal law that prohibits balance-billing and unexpected out-of-network charges. This development is significant for revenue-cycle teams as it clarifies how organizations must navigate dispute resolution requirements and manage compliance with the No Surprises Act's core dispute mechanisms.

also.title: Trump administration reforms surprise billing dispute resolution - Healthcare Dive

items.title: Congressional Budget Office contends provider wins undermining No Surprises Act - Radiology Business
items.reason: This item directly addresses litigation and enforcement outcomes related to the No Surprises Act, specifically discussing Congressional Budget Office findings about provider legal victories that are undermining the law's implementation.
items.summary: The Congressional Budget Office has raised concerns that recent provider legal wins are eroding the effectiveness of the No Surprises Act's balance-billing protections. These provider victories are reportedly enabling healthcare facilities to circumvent No Surprises Act safeguards, reducing the law's intended consumer protection impact. The CBO's position indicates that litigation outcomes favoring providers are creating gaps in federal balance-billing enforcement that affect how the law functions in practice. This development is significant for revenue-cycle teams as it suggests the legal landscape around No Surprises Act compliance may be shifting in ways that alter enforcement strategies and claims-handling procedures.

also.title: AHIP cites 'provider-driven' abuse in IDR process - Healthcare Finance News

items.title: Federal Independent Dispute Resolution Operations
items.reason: This Federal Register document directly addresses final rules on core IDR operational mechanics including disclosure requirements, claim coding standards, open negotiation periods, eligibility review, fee structures, bundled payments, and batching—all central to how the federal IDR process functions.
items.summary: CMS/HHS/DOL issued final rules in the Federal Register amending the federal IDR process under the No Surprises Act, with changes affecting multiple operational components. Plans and issuers must now disclose specific information with initial payments or denials using standardized claim adjustment reason codes (CARCs) and remittance advice remark codes (RARCs) when communicating with out-of-network entities. The final rules also modify requirements for the open negotiation period, IDR initiation, eligibility review procedures, administrative and certified IDR entity fee structures, and introduce a formal definition of bundled payment arrangements while adjusting batching rules and timeframe extension provisions. These changes directly impact how revenue-cycle teams must process, communicate about, and manage disputes for out-of-network claims, requiring system updates to coding standards and procedural compliance across the entire IDR workflow.

items.title: Federal IDR fee drops from $115 to $15: 6 things spine and orthopedic leaders should know - beckersspine.com
items.reason: The item reports on a direct change to the federal IDR administrative fee structure—from $115 to $15—which is a concrete operational rule change that directly affects how out-of-network payment disputes are arbitrated and the costs associated with the IDR process.
items.summary: The federal IDR administrative fee has been reduced from $115 to $15 per dispute, representing a significant cost decrease for the Independent Dispute Resolution process under the No Surprises Act. This fee reduction affects spine and orthopedic providers and payers who use the IDR system to resolve out-of-network payment disagreements. The lower fee may increase the financial viability of pursuing IDR for lower-dollar disputes and could influence dispute resolution strategies for healthcare providers and payers. This change impacts how organizations evaluate the cost-benefit analysis of arbitration versus settlement in payment disputes.

also.title: A win for practices: No Surprises Act dispute fee falls to $15 - Medical Economics
